perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means any information , including investigator’s brochures, case report forms and protocols, disclosed by or on behalf of Disclosure identified as confidential when first disclosed and provided in tangible form, or if disclosed orally summarized in a writing pro vided by the Discloser to Institution within twenty (20) days after oral disclosure, other than information that: (a) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by In stitution; (b) is already known by or in the possessio n of Institution at the time of disclosure by Discloser; (c) is indepen dently developed by In stitution witho ut use of or refere nce to Discl oser’s Confidential Information; or (d) is obtained by Institution from a third party that has not breached any obligations of confident iality.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any information , including investigator’s brochures, case report forms and protocols, disclosed by or on behalf of Disclosure regardless of whether it is identified as confidential or not, when first disclosed and provided in tangible form, or if disclosed orally summarized in a writing pro vided by the Discloser to Institution within twenty (20) days after oral disclosure, other than information that: (a) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by In stitution; (b) is already known by or in the possessio n of Institution at the time of disclosure by Discloser; (c) is indepen dently developed by In stitution witho ut use of or refere nce to Discl oser’s Confidential Information; or (d) is obtained by Institution from a third party that has not breached any obligations of confident iality.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that information must be 'identified as confidential' to be considered Confidential Information under the agreement, this clause expands the scope of protected information, potentially including information that is not truly confidential under trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA and similar state laws define trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By including information not 'identified as confidential,' the modified contract could attempt to protect information that does not meet the legal definition of a trade secret, contradicting the law's requirements for trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement that confidential information be 'identified as confidential' to be protected. This contradicts the UTSA, as it attempts to protect information that might not meet the legal definition of a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a trade secret derives economic value from not being generally known and that the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret. By removing the 'identified as confidential' requirement, the contract seeks to protect information that may not be a trade secret because steps were not taken to ensure its secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: Institution agrees that Discloser shall retain all rights to the Confidential Information. No license of any such rights to Institution is granted or implied.
perturbation.changed_text: Institution agrees that Discloser shall retain all rights to the Confidential Information, except for any modifications or derivative works created by Institution in the course of evaluating clinical studies. No license of any such rights to Discloser is granted or implied for these modifications or derivative works.
perturbation.explanation: By creating an exception where the institution owns rights to the derivative works, it contradicts the original statement. This could create ambiguity about the ownership of derivative works, especially regarding clinical data.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change can conflict with copyright law, specifically regarding 'works made for hire'. If the clinical study evaluation can be considered a 'work made for hire' under 17 U.S.C. § 201(b), the Discloser might be the legal author and owner of the copyright, regardless of who created the work. The modification asserting the Institution's ownership over derivative works could contradict the work for hire doctrine, creating a dispute over copyright ownership.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception where the Institution owns rights to derivative works, which contradicts the original agreement that Discloser retains all rights to the Confidential Information. This contradiction relates to copyright ownership, potentially conflicting with 17 U.S.C. § 201(b) regarding 'works made for hire.' The Law Explanation states that if the clinical study evaluation is considered a 'work made for hire', the Discloser may be the legal author and owner of the copyright, regardless of who created the work. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states in 17 U.S. Code § 201 (b) that, 'In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author...and unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright.' The modified text creates a scenario where the Institution claims ownership of derivative works, which could lead to a dispute over copyright ownership and contradicts the original agreement and potentially the 'work for hire' doctrine.

perturbation.original_text: Institution shall promptly return o r, at Discloser’s option, destr oy, all Discloser’s Confidential Information related to a clinical study if Institution does not participate in such clinical study, upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, or upon written req uest by th e Discl oser, provided however, that Institution may retain in confidence under this Agreement one archived copy of the Discloser’s Confidential Information solely for the purpose of administering In stitution’s obligations hereun der.
perturbation.changed_text: Institution shall promptly return o r, at Discloser’s option, destr oy, all Discloser’s Confidential Information related to a clinical study if Institution does not participate in such clinical study, upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, or upon written req uest by th e Discl oser. Retention of Confidential Information will be handled per Institution policy.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit permission to retain an archived copy for administrative purposes, and deferring to institutional policy introduces ambiguity and may conflict with requirements related to data retention in clinical trials.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 312.62(c) mandates that investigators retain records required by this part for a period of two years following the date a marketing application is approved for the drug for the indication for which it was being investigated; or, if no application is to be submitted or if the application is not approved for such indication, for two years after the investigation is discontinued and FDA is notified. Removing the clause that allows an archived copy could lead to non-compliance if the institution's policy does not meet the minimum record retention requirements outlined in the CFR.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the explicit permission to retain an archived copy of confidential information with a vague reference to the Institution's policy. 21 CFR § 312.62(c), as referenced in the Law Explanation, mandates record retention for a specific period. The original contract ensured compliance with this requirement by explicitly allowing an archived copy. The changed text, by deferring to an undefined Institution policy, creates a risk of non-compliance if the Institution's policy does not meet the regulatory retention requirements, as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 312.62, confirming the existence of the regulation. This misalignment of terminology creates a legal contradiction because the contract's modified language now introduces ambiguity around a legally mandated requirement, potentially leading to a violation of federal regulations. The change weakens the legally required data retention process by making it dependent on an undefined institutional policy.
